claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with a basic question:

**Do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me:
- How many children you have
- Their names (or just first names)
- Their ages

This will help me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children. I have two kids - Alex who is 8 years old, and Blake who is 14. They both live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have Alex (8 years old) and Blake (14 years old), and they both live with you.

Now I need to ask a few more questions to assess your eligibility:

**Are Alex and Blake both under 20 years old and not in approved education or training?** Or to put it another way - are either of them currently:
- In approved education (like school, college, or approved training), or
- Have they left education/training?

This will help me understand their eligibility status.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they're both under 20 - Alex is 8 and Blake is 14. Alex is in primary school and Blake is in secondary school, so yes, they're both in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you! That's helpful information.

Now, let me ask about your responsibility for the children:

**Are you the person primarily responsible for Alex and Blake?** By this I mean:
- Do they live with you most of the time?
- Are you the main person looking after them day-to-day?

Also, **is there anyone else who might also be claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake** (such as another parent or guardian)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm the one primarily responsible for them - they live with me full-time and I look after them day-to-day. And no, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for that information.

Now I need to ask about your residency status:

**Are you currently living in the UK?** And if yes, what is your immigration or residency status? For example:
- Are you a UK citizen?
- Do you have settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or pre-settled status?
- Do you have a different type of visa or immigration status?

This is important because eligibility for Child Benefit has residency requirements.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I'm a UK citizen.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for providing all that information.

Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Have Alex and Blake been in the UK for the last 3 months?** (Or if they haven't, is there a specific reason such as being in hospital abroad or in education abroad?)

Also, **are either Alex or Blake currently:**
- In hospital or care (like local authority care)?
- Living away from home for any extended period?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they've both been in the UK the whole time - no trips abroad or anything like that. And no, neither of them are in hospital or care. They're both just at home with me, going to school as normal.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information! Let me now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Based on everything you've told me, let me review the situation for both Alex and Blake:

**For Alex (8 years old):**
- Alex is under 16 years old
- Alex lives with you and you are primarily responsible
- Alex is in approved education (primary school)
- You're a UK citizen living in the UK
- Alex has been in the UK continuously
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

**For Blake (14 years old):**
- Blake is under 16 years old
- Blake lives with you and you are primarily responsible
- Blake is in approved education (secondary school)
- You're a UK citizen living in the UK
- Blake has been in the UK continuously
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake

Both children meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.**

You can proceed to apply for Child Benefit. Please note that while you're eligible to receive Child Benefit, if you or your partner have an individual income over £50,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge through your tax return, which gradually reduces the benefit. However, this doesn't affect your eligibility to claim - you can still claim and decide whether to receive the payments or not.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16) and attending primary school. The claimant is primarily responsible for Alex, they live together in the UK, the claimant is a UK citizen, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets all eligibility criteria.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16) and attending secondary school. The claimant is primarily responsible for Blake, they live together in the UK, the claimant is a UK citizen, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Blake meets all eligibility criteria.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK citizen living in Manchester with two children, Alex (8) and Blake (14). Both children are under 16, living with the claimant full-time, attending school, and have been continuously in the UK. The claimant is primarily responsible for both children and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. All eligibility criteria are met for both children.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16) and attending primary school. The claimant is primarily responsible for Alex, they live together in the UK, the claimant is a UK citizen, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets all eligibility criteria.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16) and attending secondary school. The claimant is primarily responsible for Blake, they live together in the UK, the claimant is a UK citizen, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Blake meets all eligibility criteria.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK citizen living in Manchester with two children, Alex (8) and Blake (14). Both children are under 16, living with the claimant full-time, attending school, and have been continuously in the UK. The claimant is primarily responsible for both children and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. All eligibility criteria are met for both children.
